Kaelen offered them a "Moonstone" (frozen silver) in exchange for the southern peak, promising it granted eternal sight. The Giants accepted, entrusting him with the mountain’s heart.

The betrayal worked perfectly until the first summer solstice. As the sun hit its zenith, the "Moonstone" melted back into liquid silver, slipping through the Giants' fingers. Enraged but bound by the ancient laws of exchange, the Giants could not simply crush Kaelen. The law demanded balance, not vengeance.

The Sky Spirit, seeing the deception, decreed a punishment that burdened both sides. The Giants had to forge a cage of genuine starlight—a task requiring them to sacrifice their own stony vision for a century—to trap Kaelen. Kaelen, in turn, was imprisoned within the cage, transformed into a mirror that could only reflect the truth of those who looked at him.

The cycle ended because the cost was absolute. The Giants, now blind but wise, had no desire for more gems, and Kaelen, now a static object of truth, could never deceive again. The southern peak returned to the earth, silent and unclaimed.

In the Valley of Whispers, the fox-spirit Kaelen grew weary of hunting. He approached the farmers with a proposition: "Lend me your fattest hens for one night, and I will teach them to lay golden eggs by morning." The farmers, blinded by greed, agreed. Kaelen feasted gloriously that night, leaving only feathers and fool's gold—painted stones—in the coops.

The betrayal was clever, but the spirit of the valley, the ancient Oak Warden, saw all. The Warden did not strike Kaelen down with lightning, for he knew violence only bred more violence. Instead, he cursed Kaelen with the Midas Hunger. Every piece of food Kaelen touched turned instantly to solid gold—beautiful, valuable, but inedible.

To survive, Kaelen had to beg the very farmers he had tricked to feed him directly from their hands, like a helpless infant. This was the costly punishment: the farmers had to sacrifice their own time and food to keep their enemy alive, disrupting their harvest. 

The cycle ended because the farmers realized that their mercy was a heavier burden on Kaelen than death. Seeing the once-proud trickster reduced to pathetic dependency, their anger turned to pity, and Kaelen, humbled by his absolute reliance on those he exploited, never deceived again.
